You are not Moses without Rachel and Jacob.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And if you ever forget that, if the Israelites forget that, if you forget that, they may be able to physically leave Egypt.<|8.70|> <|9.10|> But if you forget the ever-present past, Egypt will never leave you.<|16.04|> <|17.54|> Remember your past.<|18.76|> <|20.36|> This is what Moses hears from the riveting crackle of a surprising element.<|26.08|> <|26.08|> Remember.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Crackle, crackle, the divine voice says.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I will not speak to you through the places and people<|9.00|> <|9.00|> and circumstances you find most comfortable.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> If you're at ease with water, I will speak to you by flame.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If you pilot the skies, I will speak to you as ground.<|5.64|> <|5.64|> If you are comfortable with the coast, I will speak to you from the interior.<|11.38|> <|12.96|> The God of Moses is a defier of expectations.<|17.92|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> a shunner of constraints and a disruptor of every attempt,<|5.94|> <|6.20|> every attempt that would seek to box in untamable mercy and love.<|12.70|> <|14.38|> Crackle, crackle, the divine voice says,<|17.06|> <|17.06|> my name and presence evade easy, digestible categories.<|22.80|> <|24.00|> I will be what I will be.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.12|> I will be what you need me to be. I will be who I will be. I will be what you need me to be<|16.60|> <|16.60|> when you need me to be it. It doesn't quite work in English the way it works in Hebrew.<|23.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> through.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.86|> To Hagar, this is the God who sees.<|6.12|> <|6.88|> To David, this God is the God who is a fearless companion in the valley of the shadow of death.<|14.68|> <|14.68|> To Julian of Norwich, this God makes all things well.<|19.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to the prophet Miriam, sister of Moses.<|2.64|> <|3.16|> This God creates paths in the great sea<|6.38|> <|6.38|> and is the vanquisher eventually<|8.68|> <|8.68|> of Egypt's chariots and warriors.<|11.76|> <|12.48|> To Mary, whose flesh is God's flesh<|15.62|> <|15.62|> and whose blood is God's blood,<|17.82|> <|18.26|> God is nourished at her breasts<|20.82|> <|20.82|> and works and rests in her lap."<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To Sarah, this God is the laugh that comes after a lifetime of disappointment.<|8.74|> <|10.26|> And to my maternal grandmother, this God is the rose of Sharon, the bright morning star,<|19.42|> <|20.56|> the lily of the valley, and so much more.<|24.72|> <|24.72|> So much more.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.60|><|endoftext|>
